coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon nanodots in zeolite SAPO-46 channels for Q-switched fiber laser generation
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xintong
Chen, Jiaqi
Shi, Wentao
Sun, Dalin
Chu, Shaowen
Sun, Lang
Zhai, Jianpang
Pei, Jihong
Wang, Lei
Ruan, Shuangchen
Tang, Zikang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have gained much attention due to their unique optical and physicochemical properties. Herein, C-dots with uniform size have been fabricated via an in situ pyrolysis of dipropylamine (DPA) organic amines confined in the channels of SAPO-46 (AFS). The as-obtained C-dots@AFS composite material shows nonlinear optical property, which can be used as saturable absorber (SA) to generate Q-switched ytterbium-doped fiber laser (YDFL). By inserting the C-dots@AFS SA into an YDFL cavity pumped by a 980 nm laser diode, stable passively Q-switched fiber laser can be obtained for a threshold pump power of 220 mW, and 4.5 μs pulses with a repetition rate of 33.51 kHz can be obtained for a pump power of 380 mW. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a C-dots@AFS-based Q-switched fiber laser.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zeolite
C-dots
Saturable absorber
Q-switched fiber laser

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental setup of ytterbium-doped fiber laser

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis process of C-dots@AFS composite material.

(a) Experimental (upper) and simulated (lower) XRD patterns of AFS. (b) SEM image of as-synthesized AFS crystals. (c) Framework structure of the AFS single crystal retrieved from single crystal X-ray diffraction data.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) Powder XRD pattern, (d) Raman spectrum of, (e) DLS distribution, and (f) zeta potential of as-synthesized C-dots.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) high-resolution C 1s spectrum, (c) high-resolution N 1s spectrum, and (d) high-resolution O 1s spectrum of as-synthesized C-dots.

(a) The setup of a balanced twin-detector measurement. (b) The normalized absorption of the C-dots@AFS saturable absorber as a function of pump pulse peak intensity. Dots: measured data; red line: fitting to the data. (c) A schematic diagram of the ytterbium-doped fiber laser. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Typical oscilloscope traces of the Q-switched pulse trains under different pump power.
(a) Typical optical spectrum, and (b) single pulse envelope of Q-switched YDFL at the pump power of 380 mW. (c) Output power of the Q-switched YDFL as a function of the pump power of the 980 nm LD.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon nanodots in zeolite SAPO-46 channels for Q-switched fiber laser generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Laser Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Laser Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Engineering, ATR National Defence S&T Key Laboratory, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ATR National Defence S&T Key Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Physics & Materials Engineering, Faculty of Science and Technology, University of Macau, Macau

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Applied Physics & Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have gained much attention due to their unique optical and physicochemical properties. Herein, C-dots with uniform size have been fabricated via an in situ pyrolysis of dipropylamine (DPA) organic amines confined in the channels of SAPO-46 (AFS). The as-obtained C-dots@AFS composite material shows nonlinear optical property, which can be used as saturable absorber (SA) to generate Q-switched ytterbium-doped fiber laser (YDFL). By inserting the C-dots@AFS SA into an YDFL cavity pumped by a 980 nm laser diode, stable passively Q-switched fiber laser can be obtained for a threshold pump power of 220 mW, and 4.5 μs pulses with a repetition rate of 33.51 kHz can be obtained for a pump power of 380 mW. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a C-dots@AFS-based Q-switched fiber laser.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have many fantastic advantages [1–5], such as biocompatibility, low-toxicity and photostability, which promise them a wide application prospect in bioimaging [6], ion detection [7,8], photocatalysis and energy storage [9–11]. Generally, the top-down and bottom-up approaches have been developed for the synthesis of C-dots. The top-down methods are mainly based on the synthesis of C-dots by using various macroscopic carbon structures, such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, C60 molecules and graphene [12–17]. However, most of top-down methods involving a process of chemical cutting, making the C-dots with wide distribution of size and morphology. The bottom-up methods are mainly based on the direct carbonization of organic precursor molecules into C-dots, which are favor to obtain C-dots with various surface functionalities [1]. Among the bottom-up methods, template-supported strategy may provide a promising way to precisely control the size of C-dots for their template confinement effect [18,19].

Zeolites are one of the most important groups of inorganic materials with well-confined nano pore structure, and are widely used as template to fabricate nanocarbon materials [20–24]. Recently, C-dots have been synthesized in the zeolite channels via in situ pyrolysis of organic templates [25–28]. To date, more than 200 structural types of zeolite have been synthesized by using various organic templates with different chemical compositions. Thus, zeolite template-supported strategy may provide an efficient way to synthesize C-dots with uniform size and various compositions.

Q-switching is one of techniques for generate short pulse laser with high energy and have many attractive applications in telecommunications, medicine and material processing [29–31]. Many carbon materials have been used as saturable absorber (SA) to generate short pulse laser for their nonlinear optical absorption, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene [32–35]. However, there are many challenges associated with the carbon nanotubes and graphene for photonic devices. The carbon nanotubes are composited with polymer when they were used as saturable absorber, which is inclining to aggregation for their dispersion instability. Thus, the nonsaturable absorption losses will be high. Moreover, the pulsed fiber lasers can only operation in low pump power for their low thermal damage. For graphene, it still needs to the improvement their modulation depth for their wide application in pulsed fiber laser generation. Thus, it is important to find new carbon-based SA materials with nonlinear optical properties for pulsed laser generation. As a number of carbon-based nanomaterials, the nonlinear properties of C-dots have also been reported. For instance, C-dots exhibit two-photon absorption property when excited by 800 nm laser [36]. Moreover, third-order nonlinear optical properties of C-dots have also been reported [37,38]. Recently, passively Q-switched solid state laser that operation at 1 μm have been realized by using C-dots as SA [39]. However, research in this area is still highly limited.

In this paper, C-dots with uniform size have been fabricated by the pyrolysis of DPA that confined in the channels of zeolite SAPO-46 (AFS) single crystals. The C-dots@AFS material exhibit nonlinear optical property and can be used as SA to generate Q-switched ytterbium-doped fiber laser. The advantages of C-dots@AFS that used as SA are (1) improving the stability of pulsed fiber laser, which can last for six months. The zeolite channels offer a solid nano-space to fix C-dots and prevent the aggregation behavior of single C-dots, and (2) making this photonic device easy to handle, the pulsed fiber laser can be generated by putting one single crystal that hosting of C-dots between two fiber connectors to form a fiber-compatible SA, and (3) the pulsed fiber laser can operate in a high pump power with 380 mW for their high thermal damage.

All the raw chemicals, including aluminum tri-isopropoxide (Al(OiPr)3, 98%), fumed silica (SiO2, 99%), orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85%), dipropylamine (DPA, 99%), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 48%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical, Inc., and used without further purification.

The zeolite AFS single crystals were hydrothermal synthesized in a reaction system of SiO2-Al(OiPr)3-H3PO4-DPA-HF-H2O. Typically, aluminum tri-isopropoxide and fumed silica were dispersed in a solution of orthophosphoric acid in deionized water and stirred vigorously for 6 h. Then, the DPA was added dropwisely into the gel solution and stirred for another 2 h. At last, the diluted HF solution was added into the homogeneous slurry and stirred for another 2 h. Thus, a starting gel with composition of 0.6SiO2:1.0Al2O3:1.3P2O5:3.2DPA:600H2O:1.8HF was obtained, which was sealed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C under autogenous pressure for 50 h. The as-synthesized crystals were washed with distilled water and dried at 80 °C overnight.

The as-synthesized AFS crystals were loaded into a quartz boat and heated from room temperature to 400 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min under vacuum condition. Then, C-dots@AFS composite material was obtained by holding the temperature of 400 °C for 3 h. The isolated C-dots were obtained by removing AFS template through chemical treatment. Typically, the as-synthesized C-dots@AFS sample was dissolved in hot 2 M NaOH aqueous solution followed by concentrated HCl under ultrasonic treatment. Then, the dark colored solution was washed with deionized water several times and the insoluble materials were centrifugally separated from the solution.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance instrument with a Cu Kα source. The morphology of the AFS was studied using a scanning electron microscope (JEOL Co., Model: JSM-6049LA). The single crystal X-ray diffractometry (Xcalibur, Sapphire 3, Gemini ultra) was used to characterize the structure of crystal at temperature of 173 K. The enhanced Cu target was used as the X-ray source. Lattice determination and data collection was carried out by using CrysAlisPro Version 1.171.36.28 software. Absorption correction was also performed by the same package with empirical absorption correction using spherical harmonics, implemented in SCALE3 ABSPACK scaling algorithm. Structure solution and refinement were performed by using SHELXS-97 software packages. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements for the detail microstructure were executed on FEI TECNAI G2 60-300 equipped with an image spherical aberration (Cs) at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Raman spectrum was obtained using Horiba Jobin-Yvon LabRan HR800 with 514.5 nm laser excitation. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was performed in aqueous solution using a HORIBA Zetasizer LB-550V apparatus at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were recorded on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Themo-Fisher Scientific) with Al Kα (1486.6 eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup of ytterbium-doped fiber laser
ce:section.ce:para: The laser setup of ytterbium-doped fiber laser (YDFL) is shown in Fig. 4. A 980 nm laser diode (LD) was used as the pump power source and launched into the cavity through a 980/1060 nm wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) coupler. A 2.2-m-long heavily ytterbium-doped fiber (YDF) was used as gain medium. An optical isolator (ISO) was inserted into the ring cavity to ensure the unidirectional laser operation. A polarization controller (PC) was used to tune the optical polarization state in the cavity. The C-dots@AFS SA was inserted into the cavity to induce the Q-switched laser operation. A fused 10/90 output coupler was used and the 10% light was extracted from the cavity for laser property detection. The total cavity length was about 8.4 m. The output lasers were analyzed by using an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA) with a resolution of 0.02 nm, a 1 GHz high-speed oscilloscope with a photodetector, and a power meter, respectively.

Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis process of C-dots@AFS composite material. Firstly, the AFS crystals were hydrothermal synthesized by using dipropylamine as organic template in a reaction system of SiO2-Al(OiPr)3-H3PO4-DPA-HF-H2O at 200 °C for 50 h. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of as-synthesized sample matches well with the simulated XRD pattern of AFS and other reported XRD pattern of AFS [40,41], indicating the successful synthesis of pure AFS crystals with high crystalline (Fig. 2 a). Fig. 2b shows the corresponding scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of AFS crystals, which presents well-shaped hexagonal prism morphology with average size of 180 μm. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction measurement shows that the as-synthesized AFS single crystal are of P63 cm symmetry with lattice constants a = b = 13.295 Å and c = 26.742 Å, α = β = 90°, γ = 120° and their unit volume is V = 4093.56 Å3 (Fig. 2c). Then, the C-dots@AFS composite material can be obtained after carbonization at 400 °C for 3 h. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) shows the isolated C-dots after the removal of AFS template, which is mono-dispersed with a diameter of 1.5–4 nm (Fig. 3 a). The high-resolution TEM image reveals the well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphic (Fig. 3b) [25]. The powder XRD of isolated C-dots shows broad peak at 26°, indicating consist of graphene-like structures in C-dots (Fig. 3c) [5,10]. Fig. 3d shows the Raman spectrum of as-synthesized C-dots. The typical D-band (1345 cm−1) and G-band (1590 cm−1) can be clearly observed, which is similar to other reported C-dots [5,10]. The diameter of carbon dots measured by DLS is 1.5–4.5 nm, which agree well with that observed by TEM measurement, as shown in Fig. 3e. The zeta potential of carbon dots is −24.7 mV (Fig. 3f), which indicates the negative charge nature of the surface of C-dots. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were used to confirm the composition of C-dots. The C 1s peak at 284.7 eV, N 1s peak at 400 eV, and O 1s peak at 532 eV are clearly observed in the full XPS survey (Fig. 4a). The high-resolution of C 1s spectrum of C-dots can be decomposed into three main peaks of CC/CC (284.7 eV), CN/CO (286.4 eV) and CO (289.1 eV), respectively (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c shows the high-resolution of N 1s spectrum, which can be decomposed into two peaks of pyrrolic type (399.7 eV) and graphitic type (401.2 eV) N atoms. The O 1s spectrum can be decomposed into peaks of OC bonds (531.2 eV) and OC bonds (532.6 eV), as shown in Fig. 4d. These results confirm that the C-dots contain N/O-based functional groups [42,43].

In order to study the nonlinear optical property of the as-synthesized C-dots@AFS composite, a power-dependent absorption measurement based on a balanced twin-detector system was used, as shown in Fig. 5 a (using a 1040 nm probe laser with the pulse width of 460 ps and the repetition rate of 25.3 MHz). The data for normalized absorption can be well fitted according to a simple two-level saturable model [33,44]. From the fit, the modulation depth (saturable absorption) and saturable intensity are given to be 8.5% and 10.1 MW cm−2, respectively (Fig. 5b). Thus, this finding clearly illustrates that the C-dots@AFS composite have potential can be used as saturable absorber for short pulse laser generation. Fig. 5c shows the experimental setup of ring cavity of passively Q-switched ytterbium-doped fiber laser (YDFL). The as-synthesized C-dots@AFS SA was placed between two fiber connectors to integrate into the YDFL ring cavity. The continuous wave starts at the pump power of 200.5 mW, and then transits into the Q-switched operation by increasing the pump power to 220 mW (Fig. 6 a). By further increasing the pump power, the stable Q-switched pulse laser with different repetition rates is obtained, which is the typical feature of passively Q-switched fiber laser (Fig. 6b–d). However, the Q-switched pulse laser becomes unstable once the pump power is larger than 380 mW, which could be induced by the over-saturation of the SA at high pump power intensity. To exclude the thermal damage of the SA, the pump power is decreased from 420 mW to 0, and the stable Q-switched laser operation is obtained again with the pump power ranged from 380 to 220 mW, indicating the undamaged of the C-dots@AFS SA. Fig. 7 a shows the emission spectrum of the Q-switched YDFL for a pump of 380 mW. The center wavelength is at 1043.89 nm and the 3-dB bandwidth is about 1.19 nm. The corresponding pulse duration is about 4.5 μs (Fig. 7b). Fig. 7c shows the output power of the Q-switched YDFL as a function of the pump power. By increasing the pump power from 220 to 380 mW, the output power increases almost linearly from 0.2 to 5.6 mW, and the corresponding optical-to-optical efficiency is about 3.1%.

Zeolite SAPO-46 (AFS) single crystals were hydrothermally synthesized, and were used as template for in situ fabrication of C-dots by pyrolysis of DPA organic amines. The as-synthesized C-dots@AFS material can be used as saturable absorber for the generation of Q-switched fiber laser due to its nonlinear optical property. This work provides a facile method for the fabrication of C-dots@AFS composite of nonlinear optical property through in situ carbonization of confined DPA organic amines, which can serve as saturable absorber for laser generation, highlighting the potential application of C-dots@zeolite for ultrafast photonics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFA0401100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61575129, 61705134), Shenzhen Science and Technology Project (No. JCYJ20160328144942069), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M612723).
